title: lichen_l2.c: No bounds check on ipv6_len returned by lichen_link_rx()
description:
## Dataflow trace

**Source:** `lichen_link_rx()` return value via `peer_try_all_pubkeys()` (lichen_l2.c:1038-1039)

**Sink:** `net_pkt_write(pkt, rx_ipv6_buf, ipv6_len)` (lichen_l2.c:1081)

## Issue

The code passes `ipv6_len = sizeof(rx_ipv6_buf)` (240 bytes) to `lichen_link_rx()` as an in/out parameter. The function returns the decompressed IPv6 packet size in `ipv6_len`.

However, there is no validation that the returned `ipv6_len` is bounded by the original buffer size before using it:

```c
ipv6_len = sizeof(rx_ipv6_buf);  // 240 bytes
ret = peer_try_all_pubkeys(..., rx_ipv6_buf, &ipv6_len, ...);
// No check that ipv6_len <= sizeof(rx_ipv6_buf) here
...
net_pkt_write(pkt, rx_ipv6_buf, ipv6_len);  // potential overread
```

If `lichen_link_rx()` has a bug or is fed malformed data that causes it to return `ipv6_len > sizeof(rx_ipv6_buf)`, then `net_pkt_write()` will read beyond the buffer.

## Impact

SECURITY: Buffer overread. Could leak stack/heap memory contents into network packets or trigger undefined behavior.

## Fix

Add a bounds check after the call returns:

```c
if (ipv6_len > sizeof(rx_ipv6_buf)) {
    LOG_ERR("CRITICAL: lichen_link_rx returned len %zu > buffer %zu - corruption likely",
            ipv6_len, sizeof(rx_ipv6_buf));
    k_panic();  // or return early
}
```

This mirrors the safety check at lora_l2.c:262-267 for `lora_recv()`.

## Notes

This is a defense-in-depth issue. The `lichen_link_rx()` implementation should properly bound its output, but the caller should not blindly trust external return values for memory operations.
